[...] After some time, then, they were made disobedient by the women whom you saw clothed in black raiment, who had their shoulders bare, and their hair loose, and were beautiful. When they saw them they desired them, and put on their power, and put off the clothing and power of the maidens. They were therefore rejected from the house of God and were handed over to those women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule of expulsion: even those who have attained the 'name' (citizenship/standing) can be rejected and lose their place in the 'house of God' (the Church) if they adopt specific forbidden behaviors/associations.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to members of the community who have already been admitted but then violate the internal codes of conduct.

proof: [§730] They will enter, said he, if they put away the works of these women, and take back the power of the maidens and walk in their deeds. For this cause also there was a pause in the building, in order that, if they repent, they may go away into the building of the tower.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author outlines a concrete path for those previously expelled to regain their standing and resources within the community, provided they meet specific criteria of 'repentance' and 'deeds.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific sub-set of the population: those who were members, were expelled, and now seek re-entry.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the Kingdom of God
proof: [§744] They had need, said he, to come up through the water that they might be made alive, for they could not, otherwise enter into the kingdom of God, unless they put away the mortality of their former life. So these also who had fallen asleep received the seal of the Son of God and entered into the kingdom of God. [...] The seal, then, is the water.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes 'the seal' (identified as water/baptism) as a concrete legalistic requirement for transitioning from a 'dead' state to 'life' within the community and the 'Kingdom'. Without this specific ritual act, access is barred regardless of previous righteousness.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all 'nations under heaven' or the 'twelve tribes' representing the whole world.

proof: [§760] For this reason, those who have no knowledge of God and do wickedly, are condemned to death, but those who have knowledge of God and have seen his great deeds, and do wickedly, shall be punished doubly, and shall die for ever.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific stratification regarding penal consequences: those with 'knowledge' (members of the group) are subject to twice the severity of punishment for the same wicked acts compared to those outside the group.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those who have attained 'knowledge' or the 'seal', a large but specific subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain in the Church
proof: [§757] But after they entered in together and became one body, some of them defiled themselves and were cast out from the family of the righteous, and became again what they had been before, or rather even worse.
[§761] so the Church of God also shall be, after it has been purified, and the wicked and hypocrites and blasphemers and double-minded, and doers of various wickedness, have been rejected from it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the systematic removal (casting out/rejection) of individuals from the 'body' or 'family of the righteous' based on their failure to maintain group standards, reverting their status to that of outsiders.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects individuals within the community who deviate from specific behavioral norms.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to seek legal or communal pardon
proof: [§764] From the first mountain, the black one, are such believers as these: apostates and blasphemers against the Lord, and betrayers of the servants of God. For these there is no repentance, but there is death, and for this cause they also are black, for their race is lawless.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a categorical rule for 'apostates' and 'betrayers' within the community: they are denied the possibility of repentance (a process of restoration) and are instead subject to 'death', indicating a total loss of standing and life within the community's framework.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of former believers categorized by their change in status (apostasy) and betrayal.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to seek restoration through repentance
proof: [§767] For this reason, said he, there is repentance for these, because they did not deceive their Lord and were not betrayers of the servants of God; but because of the lust of gain, they played the hypocrite, and each taught according to the lusts of sinful men. But they will be punished in some way, yet repentance is open to them because they did not become blasphemers or traitors.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly differentiates teachers who taught for profit/hypocrisy from traitors. Unlike the first group, these professionals maintain the right to 'repentance' (legal/communal restoration), though they remain subject to 'some way' of punishment.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This centers on a specific occupational and functional role within the early Christian community.

extracted_rules.resource: Ease of access to high communal status
proof: [§768] Of them are those who are rich and are mixed up with many affairs of business, for the thistles are the rich... Such then (will enter with difficulty the kingdom of God.’ For just as it is difficult to walk with naked feet among thistles, so it is also difficult, for such men to enter into the Kingdom of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Economic status (wealth) is treated here as a concrete barrier to 'entering the Kingdom of God', which in this context functions as the ultimate political/spiritual citizenship in the community's framework.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all wealthy individuals involved in business affairs.

proof: [§792] The Lord, therefore, seeing their simplicity and all their innocence, filled them with the labours of their hand, and was gracious to them in all their doings.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic reward for those who live 'simple and guileless' lives; they are granted success in their 'labours of their hand', which equates to material prosperity/economic fruitfulness within the society's value system.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific moral/dispositional class that behaves with consistent simplicity.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to belong to the communal structure
proof: [§786] But those who have great cracks are those who are persistent in their evil-speaking, and are become malicious in their rage against one another. These then were cast away from the tower and were rejected from its building.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Individuals who persist in social conflict ('evil-speaking', 'malice') are systematically 'cast away' and 'rejected' from the 'tower' (the community structure), representing a loss of citizenship and belonging.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a specific subset of people engaged in persistent intra-community conflict.

proof: [§797] Those with spots are ministers who ministered amiss, and devoured the living of widows and orphans, and made gain for themselves from the ministry which they had received to administer.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the 'devouring' of the living of widows as a grave transgression for ministers, implying a social and religious rule that these groups' resources must be protected by those in authority.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Widows represent a specific but significant legal and social category within the early Christian community and broader Roman society.

proof: [§799] Those with spots are ministers who ministered amiss, and devoured the living of widows and orphans, and made gain for themselves from the ministry which they had received to administer.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The condemnation of ministers who profit from the resources of orphans indicates a rule of communal protection for the property of fatherless children.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Orphans are a specific group characterized by age and loss of paternal protection.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold office and social status
proof: [§806] Bishops and hospitable men who at all times received the servants of God into their houses gladly and without hypocrisy; and the bishops ever ceaselessly sheltered the destitute and the widows by their ministration, and ever behaved with holiness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines the role of a Bishop by the concrete obligation to provide shelter and resources to the destitute and widows. If they perform these duties, they retain their 'glorious' status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Bishops are a small, elite leadership group within the society.

proof: [§808] and the bishops ever ceaselessly sheltered the destitute and the widows by their ministration, and ever behaved with holiness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic practice where the destitute have a recognized claim upon the hospitality and resources of the community's leaders (bishops).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The 'destitute' encompasses a large portion of the lower strata of the ancient world.

proof: [#818] If the heathen punish their servants, if one deny his lord, what think you will the Lord, who has power over all, do to you?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author references the real-world legal and customary practice of 'heathen' (non-Christian) masters punishing their slaves (servants) for denial or disobedience as a baseline for his analogy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Slaves were a massive demographic and the rule of master-over-slave was foundational.

proof: [§817] Confess that you have a Lord, lest you deny him and be delivered into prison. [§818] If the heathen punish their servants, if one deny his lord, what think you will the Lord, who has power over all, do to you?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author refers to the contemporary risk of Christians being 'delivered into prison' by the secular authority ('the heathen') for their confession, reflecting the legal vulnerability of that group in 150 AD.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those brought under questioning by Roman authorities.

proof: [§824] he commanded their wealth to be cut down, yet not to be wholly taken away from them, that they may be able to do some good with that which was left them... Therefore they were cut down a little, and placed in the building of this tower.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary/religious expectation that the wealthy must have their riches 'cut down' (likely through charity or confiscation for communal use) to be considered useful members of the community.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the segment of the community possessing surplus wealth.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to the kingdom of God
proof: [§830] But this world and the vanities of their riches must be cut away from them, and then they will be meet for the kingdom of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that riches are an obstacle to entering the kingdom of God and must be 'cut away' before a person is considered 'meet' or fit for entry, indicating a systematic exclusion based on the retention of material wealth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a broad socioeconomic class (the wealthy) within the religious community.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from punishment for others' failings
proof: [§835] But he will rejoice if all be found whole; but if he find some of them fallen away, it will be woe to the shepherds. [§836] But if the shepherds themselves be found fallen away, what shall they answer to the Master of the flock? That they have fallen away because of the sheep? They will not be believed, for it is incredible that a shepherd should be harmed by the sheep, and they will rather be punished for their lie.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the early church described by Hermas, 'shepherds' (leaders/clergy) are held strictly accountable for the spiritual state of their 'sheep' (laity). They are denied the defense of claiming the laity led them astray and face specific punishment for the failures of those under their charge.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those in leadership positions within the community.

proof: [§847] Whoever therefore knows the distress of such a man, and does not rescue him, incurs great sin and becomes guilty of his blood. Therefore do good deeds, all you who have learnt of the Lord, lest the building of the tower be finished while you delay to do them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a mandatory customary rule where those with the means ('all you who have learnt of the Lord' and are 'able to do right') are required to rescue those in 'distress' or 'destitute' circumstances. Failure to do so is treated as a capital moral offense ('guilty of his blood'), implying a loss of absolute right to retain one's goods in the face of another's survival needs.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It applies to all members of the community who possess the resources to help others.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in the sacred building
proof: [§847] Unless therefore you hasten to do right the tower will be finished and you will be shut out.
extracted_rules.reasoning: A concrete deadline is imposed on those who delay mandatory 'good deeds' (almsgiving/distress relief). Once the 'tower' (the community/church structure) is complete, they lose the right to be included ('shut out').
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general threat of exclusion applied to any member who fails to meet behavioral/economic standards before a specific deadline.

proof: [§863] Whoever therefore knows the distress of such a man, and does not rescue him, incurs great sin and becomes guilty of his blood. Therefore do good deeds, all you who have learnt of the Lord, lest the building of the tower be finished while you delay to do them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a strict communal rule within the early Christian society where failing to use one's resources to rescue a person in 'distress' or 'necessity' (poverty) is equated with blood-guilt. This creates a concrete social and religious obligation to redistribute wealth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all members of the community who have the 'ability to do right' (resources), establishing a broad expectation of charity.

proof: [§865] But I say that every man ought to be taken out from distress, for he who is destitute and suffers distress in his daily life is in great anguish and necessity. [§866] Whoever therefore rescues the soul of such a man from necessity gains great joy for himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that 'every man ought to be taken out from distress,' framing the relief of the destitute as a mandatory communal action rather than a vague suggestion, effectively granting the poor a claim on the community's resources.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The group 'the destitute' is a large segment of the ancient Roman urban population to which Hermas belonged.

proof: [§857] He said to the shepherd: I know that the servant of God wishes to live, and will keep these commandments, and will provide for the maidens in purity. When he had said this he handed me over again to the shepherd, and called the maidens and said to them: Since I see that you willingly dwell in his house I commend him and his house to you, that you depart not at all from his house.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text depicts a customary arrangement where the head of the household is commanded to 'provide for the maidens' in exchange for their spiritual/moral presence. This implies a formal obligation of the host to provide material support (housing and food) to these specific women.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a very specific group of 'maidens' (likely representing virtues or a specific class of virgins within the church) attached to a specific household type.
